data.text:
INT: Tape 18, with Mark Verstandig. We were talking about the new hiding place for your wife. 

MV: [NON-ENGLISH]. 

INT: Yes. 

MV: We arrived there during the night. And next day, in the morning, next day in the morning, Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] came to tell me that, unfortunately, we will have a visitor tonight. That opposite-- there was a house opposite, and he is a-- he's a [NON-ENGLISH], or a deputy [NON-ENGLISH], something like that, an official. 

And he asked me about you, because he saw two people during the night coming to my house, and only one went back. So he's bright and a dangerous man. And he wanted-- he invited himself to Christmas dinner. 

It was Christmas Eve. In Poland, on Christmas Eve was the Christmas dinner, not a day later. He invited himself to Christmas dinner, so you should be careful. Now, and the arrangement was that there was a cupboard, which stood a little bit on a slant. And when visitors came, my wife went in-- behind the cupboard. 

Of course, it was cold. She couldn't come up. She wouldn't go up to the loft, because she would be freezing to death. So she stood behind-- she stood behind this cupboard when people came in. 

So he said, look, your wife will go behind the cupboard. And you will have to sing all the carols, together. He told his-- his neighbor that I am an officer of the Polish army, and I was the officer of this company, where his son, his eldest son, was serving. 

And he accepted that. So he gave the-- the-- the-- they tutored me, the whole afternoon, to sing carols. I knew-- so I knew the tunes, since I knew some carols, but, anyhow, I went through this tuition. And-- and I went through all this, his stories about the army life and about the place where they were serving in the army, and so on, that-- that I should know about it. 

But it started with a-- it started with a surprise, because when-- when-- when-- when people-- when people gathered before the dinner, they all stood up to receive altar bread. I didn't know what to do. But I was, fortunately, in the middle. I could see what the others were doing. I did the same, so I passed the exam. 

Well, what was very-- very unfortunate, they forgot to tell me how-- how the dinner will start. And we were-- we were drinking bimber the whole night. And we're talking about the army life and about the war and about the prospects and what's going on in Africa and the [INAUDIBLE], everything, politics, everything. 

And we are drinking and drinking. I-- from time to time, you-- we-- I-- I stood up, and I went out, like old people drinking a lot. We went out. But when I went out, I put my fingers into my mouth and I threw out-- I vomited-- I threw out all the bimber, which I had before, together with the food. And then I came back. 

At one stage, when I came back, he told me, oh, my friend, he told me, you are white like a ghost. And I-- I entered the room back. And I had my lessons from Jandrush, from my friend, Jasinszki. He told me, when you are in trouble, and you feel that you-- that-- that your blood is going-- is-- is-- is running away from your face, bend down, correct your shoelaces, and the blood will come back to your face. 

So I remembered this immediately. I bent down, I corrected my laces, and I came back saying-- I said, look, my friend, am I white? Oh, shit. Oh, my god. No. He said, look, you drank too much. You piss off, you see? Just like them. He apologized. 

Anyhow, we were drinking till 4 o'clock in the morning. The whole family, the old [NON-ENGLISH], his wife, his daughter, his sons, they all were lying on the floor dead. They all were drunk, dead. The only people who was not drunk, that was me and this man, who was drunk, too. He-- he was drunk, but I was not. 

And then it was-- and my wife was standing, all the time, behind the cupboard. So at that moment, we decided it's time to go home. When we went out, he told me, I tell you a secret. When I came in this afternoon, I thought I will find a Jew, and I will send him to hell, where they all belong, and I sent a good few Jews there. 

And I thought for-- for a while, it would be high time to-- to take this fellow not to his house, but a little bit to the left, where the-- where the Vistula is, where the river is. Where the-- where the river is. And there are holes made in the ice. In the ice, holes are being made, all-- all-- a few-- every few hundred yards for the fish to-- to-- to get air, and-- and to throw him into such a hole. 

But then I decided, I never killed anybody. I never hit a dog. And I don't know that I will be able to go through killing him in. And besides this, I thought-- I thought, too, there will be traces next day in the morning, two people going to the river and one-- and one going back. But besides, I-- I-- I thought, I will not be able to-- I will not be able to do it. 

I took him to his house. And look, he kissed me. He was so friendly. And that was the end of that-- of-- of that beautiful dinner, of the Christmas dinner. I was-- we were there until about-- about-- at least three months, maybe-- no. Almost-- we-- we-- March-- maybe through till March. 

In March, they called in Kubick and told him, look, they are very nice people, but I think people may start thinking that we are hiding somebody, because we are not inviting anybody anymore. 

We have to open our house. And we wouldn't like that something should happen to those people in our house. So find for them another place, and take them away. If you wish, in a few months time, you can bring them back. 

So that was when we called in Kubick. And Kubick called-- contacted a certain man called Marcin Valas. That is the man who found the hiding places for my aunt and my cousins and for my sisters. They-- he went over to Valas and he came-- went together with Valas to this house, and off we went to Chrzastow. 

He prepared the hiding place at his sister's place, a family called Markowski. Very nice people. As a matter of fact, the [NON-ENGLISH] were very nice people, too, were religious people. Religious people. But they were-- were-- were very nice people. And they called the-- he-- he brought us to his sister and this-- and his brother-in-law and their family. And there was a hiding place prepared, a very interesting hiding place, because it was really-- they had the stone, which was-- which had D-- how do you call it? Looks like that. 

INT: L-shaped? 

MV: Hmm? 

INT: L-shaped. 

MV: An L shape. An L-shaped stone. The first-- the first part was-- the-- the-- the entrance was from-- from the hall, and the-- and the other part was behind the kitchen wall. So they cut off-- they cut off this stone, making it just a-- an ordinary stone, no more L shape. 

And the entrance was through the loft. It was all open through the loft. It was dark, and that was the only way they could have us. It was a very-- it was a good hiding place, a good hiding place for people, who didn't know before that there was an L shape. 

And-- and we were there for quite a-- for quite a time. And we were very happy there, because the Markowski's were very nice people. We were eating what they were eating. We never went hungry. And they treated us very, very well. We paid some monthly, or weekly, or monthly, I don't remember. And besides this, I promised them a few acres of land from Sadkowa Góra after the war. 

And one day-- one day, we were sitting. It was-- it was Christmas. No Christmas, it was Easter. It was Easter. And from the morning, we could hear the church bells from [NON-ENGLISH], from Chorzelów. It was a-- it was a church, Chorzelów, two and a half kilometers away. 

We could hear the bells ringing. You see, the Easter-- Easter bells, the resurrection of Christ, the joy, the resurrection of Christ. We were sitting on the-- in-- in the loft, the attic there. 

And suddenly, we heard screams and a cart moving slowly. And a girl crying, I don't want to be killed. What I have-- what I have done to you? I don't-- I'm young. I'm 16 years old. I don't want to be killed. They are going to kill me. They are going to kill us, and so on. 

It was very-- oh, you could see. And then, we could-- on the other hand, we could hear-- we could hear shouting people, kill them, kill them. We ran down to the housing-- hiding place. In the meantime, the cart went away-- went-- the cart went further on, so we couldn't hear already any-- any screams. But we went down. 

And a minute later, Markowski jumped up, came up, and said, Mary, Jesus, Maria. He said, Jesus, they took the [NON-ENGLISH] and her daughter. They chased them. They chased them in the field until they got them. And they-- and they took them-- and they took them to be killed. 

Now, that was Easter. You see? The bells, the church bells were ringing joy and the resurrection of Christ and forgiveness. And here, they took a father and his 16-year-old daughter to be killed. 

INT: Easter '43? 

MV: Hmm? 

INT: Easter '43? 

MV: Easter '43. Easter '43. As a matter of fact, it was, at that time, a few days later, I got the news that my sisters were killed. I told them about-- I talked earlier about how they were killed in [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was wondering, what happened? After all, the war-- the-- that the German's were losing the war. The Russians were coming closer and closer. 

And here-- and here, they-- they are-- they are-- the-- the chasing of the Jew, of the Jewish people became more intensive than ever. That puzzled me. I didn't know about the Delegatura. I didn't know about the [NON-ENGLISH], didn't know about the order to kill Jews. I didn't know about it. And I never suspected that such things can happen. I never suspected that such things can happen. 

Even-- they tell you later that they killed Jews after the war, because Berman was in the government or another member. In Romania and in Hungary, there were Jewish people on-- in-- in the-- in the Communist government, in the Communist government of Hungary, in the Communist government of-- of Romania. 

They had a-- they had officially, a stronger grip on the government than in Poland. And nobody killed Jews in Poland-- in-- in-- in-- in Romania after the war. Nobody killed Jews in Hungary after the war. So it's only-- it is-- it is-- it was only in Poland, the only country where they were killing Jews. 

INT: Why were the people in the country so-- 

MV: Beg your pardon? 

INT: In the country-- Why were the people in the countryside so easily influenced, do you think? 

MV: A matter of fact, they weren't. Yeah, I wouldn't say that people were like that. It is enough that the minority was influence. There was some-- there is a-- there is Kulkowski, there is a-- a historian, an AK historian, even, Dr. Kulikowski or Klukowski or something like that. 

And he pointed out that-- that-- that the people were brutalized, and they were killing Jews like vermin. He was, himself, shocked. He was, himself, shocked. And that is a historian. And again, he is quoted in-- he's quoted largely by Martin Gilbert, and he's quoted in the book issued-- in the book, [NON-ENGLISH] book, in the-- in-- in the Yad Vashem, the Yad Vashem edition, he's-- he's quoted. 

The people were brutalized, and people were chasing Jews and killed them, like vermin. That was that. And a few months-- a few-- some weeks later-- a few-- a few weeks, maybe, a few months later-- a few months later, again, Valas came to [NON-ENGLISH]. I think we'll have to leave this place and go somewhere else, because Markowski are afraid that some neighbors may be-- may suspect him of keeping Jews. 

Some people told them, look, what's going on with you? There was-- there-- there is-- there is-- your-- your-- your smoke from your chimneys-- from your chimney in the summer. And they say, smoke from your chimney, you're baking bread more than you used to. So they were-- that means that you are-- you-- you-- you-- you-- you are-- you are baking too-- you are baking bread for-- not only for your family, but for someone else. 

People were-- were checking the smoke from the chimney to assess whether in this house there are some additional mouths to be fed with bread. And Markowski said, it's high time that you should go away for a certain-- for a certain period. Because they didn't want-- they didn't want to have trouble. 

And he took us to another place. It was a place where-- my family was there. My aunt, two cousins, and their family Brenner. They were-- they-- they were-- they were relatives of my-- of my aunt. They were not my relatives. They were relatives of my aunt. Yeah, in-laws, or something like that. There were two women over there. 

[BACKGROUND NOISE] 

There were two woman. 

CREW: I'll just stop the tape for a second. 

INT: OK. We were-- you were talking about a time came where you had to move from the Markowski's because-- 

MV: Yeah. Now, we went over there. There was a certain family called Kolciak. They had a house not on the main road but a side road, where people are not watching the smoke in the chimney. It's far away, so no houses all around there. So it's-- it's just-- just on a side road, deep in a side road. 

INT: Close to which town? 

MV: What's that? 

INT: Close to which town? 

MV: It was the same village. It was the same village. 

INT: And where was that village? What's the closest-- 

MV: That was-- that village was about seven kilometers from Mielec. So we went over there, and there was my family, my aunt, those two-- two cousins, and family Brenner, two-- two-- two middle-age woman and a boy, a 12-year old boy. 

They had an excellent-- an excellent hiding place. A much better one than here, because they had a long shed, brick-- a brick shed, a long-- for horses-- for their horses and for their cows. And at one end, they shortened the-- they shortened the shed with 75 centimeters, erecting a new wall, a new brick wall, and put the cows against this new wall. 

And underneath, there was one stone, which was-- which you could remove. So you crawled in between the cows. You crawled into this hiding place. And there was a hiding place the-- the-- the width of the-- of the shed, with two bank-- with two bunks on both sides, and a little opening or one brick opening for air. 

That was the hiding place, which means that even people who were familiar with this building would not have recognized that the building was shortened with 75 centimeters. And there we were. 

But the difference between the two families was worse. The Markowski's were very friendly, decent people. The Kolciak were-- put it that way-- ugly people. We had to pay them monthly or weekly. I don't remember. I think monthly or weekly. 

They didn't want any promises of any-- of any acres after the war. And that alone was depressing, because it looks that they don't believe that we will survive the war. And what was very important, they didn't give us enough to eat. We were starving. 

They-- they fed-- they-- they were feeding their pigs, and they were feeding their cows. It was a rich-- it was a rich peasant. But as far as we are concerned, we were just starving. Valas used to come to us every few days, during the night. 

But it would have been-- it would have been, first of all, impossible for Valas to bring food, because in case somebody meets him or somebody sees him carrying something during the night, he would follow him. And he would-- he-- he would endanger himself. He would endanger us by carrying something. 

And besides this, it would have-- it would have been in vain, because if he would have known that Valas is supplying us with some food, he would have given us even less food to start with. Well, anyhow, it was very bad. And they were very bad people. 

When good news came along, let's say Valas brought good news from the-- from the press, that the Russians are already close to the border, the Polish border, they used to say, [NON-ENGLISH]. Not one sour pill. Sour pill, you will swallow before you will be-- before-- before things will get brighter for you. They were very bad people. 

And there we were until-- for a long time. We were there until the 31st of May, 1944. At that time, the Russians were already on the-- crossing the border of Poland. And it was about 10 o'clock at night. And we heard some commotion, some movements on the yard. 

A few minutes later, Kolciak came along. He moved away the stone from the hiding place. And he said, [NON-ENGLISH]. The comedy is over. Get out. The people from the underground are waiting for you. 

We went out. We went out over there. When I came out, there were some ladies were already there before, my wife. And they took away her wedding ring. No. They didn't take away her wedding ring. They took away my wedding ring. My-- a few people were there before me. 

It was the Brenners The Brenners were there before me, and then I went in. They called me in. They called me in. They put their hands into my pockets, and they took out my-- my gray ID card. Oh, he said. You wanted to masquerade as a Pole. And I told him, look. 

And first of all, when I came in, they mishandled me. They-- they-- I got-- I got a few kicks and so on. But then they put in the hand-- it was the first time that somebody-- that somebody hit me. I'd never-- nobody hit me before. 

Then they put their hand into the pocket. They saw this gray ID. You wanted to masquerade as a Pole. I told them, look. This handwriting is the handwriting of Vladislav Jasinszki Jandrush. He gave it to me. And this is his handwriting. And I am a [NON-ENGLISH], which means an officer of the AK without commission for the time being. And he is no-- he no more alive, I told him. 

When they heard this, they gave it back to me. They went out, had a meeting, and said, you can go back to your place. Nothing will happen to you. You can stay here in peace. And they went away. 

And we were standing next to the shed outside, maybe a quarter of an hour later, and one fellow, a tall fellow, came back. And he said, look. I think we decided that you are important people, that you should come with us. We will take care of you. 

Well we went all out. There were 11 people, but nine of them went-- were in front. We all were in the middle, and two people were behind. One was a 6-foot-5 tall-- tall man with a-- with a rifle, and another one was-- was a shorty fellow with a gun, with a handgun. They looked like Pat and Patachon before the war. They were two comedians known before the war. One was a tall and one was a-- a smaller one. 

And we could see they are leading us to the next forest. The-- the forest was about, let us say, a kilometer away from this house. And we were along this side rode. 

My wife started screaming. They are going to shoot us. Was special, we saw that's not a friendly thing, because the tall man is keeping his gun ready to shoot, and so was-- so was the shorter one. They were behind us. So I told my wife not to panic. We will-- will find something. On both sides of the-- of this road were ditches, irrigation ditches. [NON-ENGLISH]. 

INT: Thank you. We will continue next tape.
